coredata.dc:title: High-impressive separation of photoinduced charge carriers on step-scheme ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon dots heterojunction with efficient activity in photocatalytic NH3 production
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Van-Huy
Mousavi, Mitra
Ghasemi, Jahan B.
Le, Quyet Van
Delbari, Seyed Ali
Shahedi Asl, Mehdi
Mohammadi, Mohsen
Shokouhimehr, Mohammadreza
Sabahi Namini, Abbas
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic N2 fixation has been overgrowing since the 20th century because the Haber–Bosch process is more energy-intensive. Therefore other sustainable alternatives are highly needed. A simple procedure for constructing novel ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots hybrid nanocomposites was exhibited through the hydrothermal approach. The surface morphology, crystal structure, and phase of the designed nanomaterials were assayed with various analyses. In this research paper, photocatalytic N2 fixation induced by ternary ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots system. The results displayed that the ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots (0.5 mL) system has remarkable activity in the N2 fixation process compared with the sole ZnO and ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%), which is 11.5 and 2.1 times, respectively. The durability of the optimum system, as an essential character of the nanocomposite, was assayed in four recycles. Moreover, the effect of solvent, electron scavenger and media pH in the NH4 + generation rate was investigated. At last, a suitable mechanism was offered for the notable N2 photofixation improvement. This novel information could clarify the construction of impressive systems for nitrogen photofixation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots
N2 fixation
Photocatalysis
S-Scheme

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Mohsen Mohammadi acknowledges the funding received from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) Grant No. RGPIN-2016–04221 . Mehdi Shahedi Asl thanks the McCain Foundation for providing enough funding through the McCain Foundation Postdoctoral Fellowship in Innovation program to conduct this work. This research was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( 2020M2D8A206983011 ). The financial support of the Basic Science Research Program ( 2017R1A2B3009135 ) through the National Research Foundation of Korea is appreciated. In addition, this work was funded by Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number of 104.05–2020.15 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) EDX spectra of as-designed optimum sample (EDX mapping of the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system (inset of Fig. 2a). (b) FESEM, (c) TEM, and (d) HRTEM image of ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system.
(a) XPS survey spectra, also XPS spectra of: (b) Zn 2p, (c) O 1 s, (d) Sn 3d, and (e) C 1 s in the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system.
NH4 + production rate for the as-constructed systems.
(a) UV–Vis DRS (insert figure: Tauc plots) of the as-constructed samples. (b) BET analyses for as-fabricated photocatalysts.
(a) PL spectra, (b) EIS curves, and (c) Transient photocurrent responses for the as-designed systems.
Mott–Schottky curve of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnSnO3 and (c) XPS valence spectra.
The offered mechanism for improving N2 reduction activity of the ZnO/ZnSnO3 system.

NH4 + production ability of the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system: (a) in the existence of DMSO, DMF, and KBrO3 (b) in presence of the air and argon gas, and (c) under various pH situations after illumination for 3 h.
(a) Durability of the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system, (b) XRD patterns, (c) and (d) SEM image of the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite before and after photocatalysis.
Schematic image of the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs construction way.
Comparison of NH4 + generation rate over the ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots (0.5 mL) nanocomposite reported in this work with other photofixation works reported in recent years. *Xe:.

head.ce:title: High-impressive separation of photoinduced charge carriers on step-scheme ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon dots heterojunction with efficient activity in photocatalytic NH3 production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic N2 fixation has been overgrowing since the 20th century because the Haber–Bosch process is more energy-intensive. Therefore other sustainable alternatives are highly needed. A simple procedure for constructing novel ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots hybrid nanocomposites was exhibited through the hydrothermal approach. The surface morphology, crystal structure, and phase of the designed nanomaterials were assayed with various analyses. In this research paper, photocatalytic N2 fixation induced by ternary ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots system. The results displayed that the ZnO/ZnSnO3/Carbon Dots (0.5 mL) system has remarkable activity in the N2 fixation process compared with the sole ZnO and ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%), which is 11.5 and 2.1 times, respectively. The durability of the optimum system, as an essential character of the nanocomposite, was assayed in four recycles. Moreover, the effect of solvent, electron scavenger and media pH in the NH4 + generation rate was investigated. At last, a suitable mechanism was offered for the notable N2 photofixation improvement. This novel information could clarify the construction of impressive systems for nitrogen photofixation.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of the global economy, humans are facing intractable crises of environmental and energy problems. A green and sustainable energy supply technology is one of the most important issues based on the friendly development of the society [1–5]. Nitrogen is a necessary element of all life. Although nitrogen is composed 78% of the total atmosphere, most plants and animals cannot directly absorb. Hence, the artificial N2 fixation method has attracted more attention from researchers in the scientific community and is introduced as the most extensive chemical reaction [6]. Recently, the Haber-Bosch process was introduced as the leading artificial N2 fixation method. In this process, in the existence of Fe-based systems, under high temperature and high pressure, N2 reacts with H2 to produce NH3. But in this process, besides high raw material prices and high energy consumption, the hazard of hydrogen cannot be ignored [7]. For this reason, lately, researchers are attempted to improve other methods, such as electrochemical [8,9], chemical [10,11], and photochemical [12,13] ways to replace the Haber-Bosch procedure.

Currently, photocatalytic as a type of advanced oxidation process (AOPs) has received more attention owing to its green cleanness, low cost, low power consumption, high efficiency, and mild conditions [14]. Up to now, many attempts have been done to improve impressive heterogeneous nanocomposites for N2 fixation, like g-C3N4 [15], BiOCl [16], Fe2O3 [17], BiOBr [18], and TiO2 [19]. Most recently, the photocatalytic conversion of dinitrogen to ammonia with high efficiency on the ZnO was assayed [20,21]. Because, from the industrial point of view, ZnO is selected as a substantial semiconductor owing to several superiorities like effective nontoxic photocatalyst, low production cost, environmental safety, and high absorption efficiency of light irradiations [22]. However, the wide optical gap of 3.2 eV makes ZnO only absorb UV part of the light, which only contains 4% of the solar spectrum. Besides, the low segregation efficiency of the charge carriers also makes its photoability not high enough. Both two drawbacks limit its photoactivity and its wide applications [23]. To overcome these limitations, many procedures like non-metal doping, photosensitive material modification, metal ion doping, and combining with semiconductors have been utilized for enhancing the photoactivity of ZnO [24–26]. To enhance the photoactivity, several research papers related to nanocomposites have been conducted in the last decades. The outcomes ascertain that combining two or more semiconductors is a new method to obtain impressive charges separation. The performance of coupled samples is better than that of single samples [27,28].

Carbon dots (CDs) are discrete quasi-spherical carbon particles with sizes of <10 nm, normally having an Sp2-conjugated carbon core with oxygenated functional groups, such as –OH, –COOH, and –CHO [29]. Since 2006, CDs have been extensively researched due to their simple synthesis, low cost, abundance, excellent biocompatibility, and other advantages and they have found diverse applications in the fields of photocatalysis, optoelectronics, energystorage/conversion, nanomedicine, sensing, and bioimaging [30]. More than half of these applications make use of the photoresponsive ability of CDs in a wide wavelength (ultraviolet to infrared) range and their outstanding electron transfer performance [31]. Because, the up-conversion feature of CDs, expands the light applying area because of converting more photons with higher wavelength to a photon with shorter wavelength through recieving photon with low energy and afterthat emitting high energy light [32]. Therefore, CDs have been applied as an efficient component in the design of photocatalysts. Based on successful coupling with semiconductor photocatalysts, the catalytic activities of CDs have been improved by inhibiting photogenerated carrier recombination and broader visible-light absorption characteristics [33,34]. Also, it is also worth noting that, since CDs are very much imagined for serving as an acceptor and donor of electron, then the displacement of the excited electrons of semiconductors by the carbon dots network can decline in the recombination of electron-hole pairs [35]. These properties guided us to focus on the combination of carbon dots with the ZnO.

Moreover, as one of the common perovskite oxides, zinc stannate (ZnSnO3) has received more attention. ZnSnO3 has wild applications in several fields, like catalysts, pyroelectric, sensors, battery studies, piezoelectric, and photoactivity. Today, these nanomaterials were prepared through different procedures such as co-precipitation, hydrothermal, solvothermal, etc., which has attracted for intriguing features investigation of the ZnSnO3 [36].

Albeit construction and evaluation about photodegradation ability of visible-light active systems based on ZnO have been lately attracted attention of researchers [37–39], in the best of our knowledge, there is no research paper about fabrication of ternary ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs nanocomposites. In this research paper, ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs systems were constructed through the hydrothermal procedure and used in the photocatalytic processes of N2 fixation by illuminating visible light. The photoactivity outcomes demonstrate that the fabricated ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs systems have high nitrogen photofixation activity. Finally, according to scavenging tests and electrochemical techniques, the plausible increased photocatalytic mechanism and the stability and reusability of the system were also investigated.

A wide variety of methods have been applied to synthesize ZnO nanostructures [40]. Most well-known ZnO nanostructures like nanorods [41], nanoneedles [42], flower [43], spindle [44], and nanobelts-like [45] structures have been synthesized by differen methods. In this work, among the synthesize methods, the ZnO were prepared via simple refluxing manner by spindle-like morphology. Typically, 5.220 g of Zn(NO3)2•4H2O was dissolved in 150 mL distilled water. Then, the aqueous solution of NaOH (5 M) was added in dropwise to the solution under stirring as far as pH of the solution reached to 10. Subsequently, the suspension was refluxed at 96 °C for 3 h. The obtained white suspension, after centrifuge and washing, dried in an oven at 70 °C for one day.

For the construction of the ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%) system, where 30% is the weight percent of ZnSnO3, 0.35 g of ZnO nanoparticles was dispersed into 150 mL distilled water for 20 min. Then, 0.154 g of Zn(NO3)2•2H2O and 0.227 g of SnCl4•5H2O were dissolved in 40 mL NaOH (0.2 M) solution, and after stirring for 60 min, sonicated for 60 min. Then, the obtained suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 11 h. The final products were washed several times with ethanol and distilled water and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h in a hot air oven.

The CDs solution was constructed according to the represented literature [46]. The ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs system was built through a straightforward procedure. Briefly, 0.4 g of the ZnO/ZnSnO3 and 0.5 mL of CDs were dispersed in 100 mL of water for 5 min. Then, the obtained suspension dried in an oven at 70 °C for one day. The schematic diagram for the construction of the nanocomposites is demonstrated in Scheme 1.
Moreover, the Characterization of photocatalysts, N2 fixation process, and electrochemical measurements are in detail in sections 2.S1 to 2.S3 of the supporting information.

The XRD patterns of as-constructed systems were observed in Fig. 1 . In XRD pattern of the ZnO sample, the peaks corresponded planes of the wurtzite hexagonal crystalline phase (JCPDS file no. 36–3411) [47]. The pattern of ZnSnO3 was in agreement with the orthorhombic perovskite ZnSnO3 crystal structure (JCPDS file no. 11–0274) [48]. Concerning the ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%) nanocomposite, it was worth noting that all the peaks of ZnO and ZnSnO3 were seen. In the case of ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system, the same patterns were seen, demonstrating that the structures of ZnO and ZnSnO3 have not been changed by the adding of CDs, and no diffraction peak of CDs was seen, which may be owing to its limited quantity in nanocomposites [46].

Moreover, the elemental composition ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system was assayed through EDX spectra and illustrated in Fig. 2 a. It demonstrates that Zn, Sn, C, and O are the elements present in the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system. The elemental mapping of Sn, Zn, O, and C exhibits the uniform distribution of four elements on the as-fabricated system inset of Fig. 2a. The morphology of the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system was assayed through the FESEM. In Fig. 2b, it is evident that the sole ZnO include oval-like nanoparticles and spherical nanoparticles of ZnSnO3, and CDs are observed around oval-like ZnO. As can be seen in the TEM image of the nanocomposite, there is a heterojunction between ZnO and ZnSnO3 and CDs nanoparticles (Fig. 2c). To more assay, the heterojunction interface between ZnO and ZnSnO3 and CDs, ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite was evaluated through the HRTEM image. The HRTEM images in Fig. 2d, provides more detailed data about the interfacial structure of the nanocomposite. Three types of lattice fringes could be observed, which allowed for the specifying of spacing of ZnO, ZnSnO3, and CDs. The segregation of the lattice planes was specified to be 0.262 nm [49], which is in good agreement with the (002) spacing of wurtzite ZnO. At the same time, the lattice fringes of 0.233 nm [50] and 0.320 nm [47] are in good agreement with the interplanar spacing of (200) and (002) planes of ZnSnO3 and CDs. Hence, the outcomes more affirmed that the heterojunction was created among ZnO, ZnSnO3, and CDs in the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system, which was in good agreement with XRD, FESEM, and EDX results.

XPS reveals elemental composition and their oxidation states in ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL). The full spectra XPS of ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) demonstrates Zn, O, Sn, and C in the system, as seen in Fig. 3 a. Zn 2p peak position at 1045.5 eV and 1022.6 eV attributed to Zn2+ (Fig. 3b) [48]. Moreover, Fig. 3c presents O 1 s spectrum. The observed distinct, prominent peal peaks at 534.0 and 532.2 eV assigned to the C = O and C–O–C groups in CDs, respectively [51]. Also, the peak of Sn 3d spectra at 486.1 and 494.5 eV in Fig. 3d exhibited the presence of Sn4+ [48]. Finally, the peaks of C 1 s, were deconvoluted into two various peaks located at 285.1 and 288.9 eV, which corresponding to COH/CO-C and C = O groups, respectively, demonstrating the existence of CDs nanoparticles (Fig. 3e) [52].
The FTIR analysis was used to study the functional group in prepared samples and the results were discussed in section 3.S1 of supporting information.

The light absorption features of the as-designed systems were assayed through UV–Vis DRS. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, the sole ZnO illustrates its visible-light photocatalytic feature with an absorption edge around 380 nm. However, after adding ZnSnO3, there is no other special absorption; the reasons may be due to the wide bandgap of ZnSnO3. Although it is shown for ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system that the absorbance of ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%) was considerably enhanced in the visible region after modified by CDs. Based on the outcomes of UV–Vis DRS, the as-prepared ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite can be expected to have excellent photocatalytic activity owing to the increased visible light absorption. Besides, the optical gap energy than specified from the (αhν)2 vs photon-energy plots (insert Fig. 5a: Tauc plots) is 3.3 eV for ZnSnO3, which is larger than that of ZnO (3.2 eV).

The surface area of as-obtained products was more assayed through N2 adsorption-desorption analysis. Fig. 5b demonstrates the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm plot of the as-designed samples. The BET surface areas of ZnO, ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%), and ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) are 5.18, 34.1, and 38.2 m2 g − 1, respectively. The outcomes exhibited that ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) possesses a larger specific surface area than pure and binary ones, which could exert an effect on their catalysis performance and adsorption.

The separation efficiency of the charges was assayed through photoluminescence(PL), EIS, and transient photocurrent response characterizations. Fig. 6 a demonstrates the PL spectra of as-designed systems. Pure ZnO demonstrates the main peak at 380 nm, which is in agreement with the other research papers. This sharp peak arises from the electron/hole pairs recombination. It could be observed that ZnSnO3 and CDs nanomaterials adhered to the ZnO demonstrated lower emission intensity than ZnO and binary nanocomposite, which might be attributed to the reduction of charges recombination. Besides, the charges migration feature also plays a main role in specifying the photoactivity of the photocatalysts. The capability of charges migration was assayed through EIS, as seen in Fig. 6b. Generally, a smaller arc size expresses a lower charge migration resistance and indicates higher charges migration efficiency. The arc size of the systems demonstrates the order of ZnO > ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%) > ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL), which is in agreement with the outcomes in Fig. 6a. Moreover, the transient photocurrent responses of as-constructed samples by on-off cycles were obtained, and the data are observed in Fig. 6c. The binary and ternary nanocomposites exhibit excellent photocurrent responses than that of bare ZnO under visible light excitation, affirming that the ZnSnO3 and CDs enhance the charges separation efficiency. The ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system demonstrates the highest photocurrent intensity. This outcome expresses that the CDs serves a pivotal role in enhancing the electron-hole pairs segregation efficiency. The data in Fig. 11 verifies that the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system demonstrates better photoactivity than that of pure and binary ones because of its enhanced charges separation efficiency.

As we know, the positions of VB and CB of components are crucial to assay the redox feature of the heterostructure. The Mott–Schottky curves of as-designed nanomaterials are observed in Fig. 7 a and b to identify the flat band potential. The ZnO and ZnSnO3 semiconductors own positive slope, indicative of n-type semiconductor [63]. In Mott–Schottky measurement, the flat band potential of ZnO and ZnSnO3 semiconductors is gained to be −0.44 and −0.16 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode, which is in corresponding to the of −0.24 and 0.04 V vs. NHE. Generally, the flat band potential is regarded as the Fermi level potential of the semiconductor. Likely, the CB of n-type semiconductors is more negative than the Fermi level potential by ~−0.1 V. Hence, CB of ZnO and ZnSnO3 semiconductors can be calculated to −0.34 and −0.06 eV, respectively. Besides, Fig. 7c demonstrates the VB-XPS spectra of ZnO and ZnSnO3 semiconductors, which are gained to be 2.86 and 3.24 eV, respectively.

Fig. 9 a shows a primary mechanism of the N2 fixation reaction (Fig. 9a). As an electron quencher, the addition of KBrO3 diminishes the N2 fixation activity of ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL). The NH4 + concentration becomes very low when adding DMSO and DMF instead of H2O. These two outcomes demonstrate that e − is the active species and that H2O creates H + for this N2 fixation process. The importance of the presence of N2 gas in the media is investigated and outcome is presented in Fig. 9b. As observed, with the replacement of air by argon gas, the NH4 + production was significantly suppressed. This experiment firmly confirmed the occurrence of N2-photofixation process, if light, photocatalyst, and N2 molecules were present in the environment. The reaction is observed in the following equation [69,70]:
N 2
+ 6
H
+
+ 6
e
−
→ 2 N
H 3

Fig. 9b demonstrates the effect of solution pH on the production of NH3. As observed, the solution pH effect on the NH3 generation was assayed (Fig. 9b). In acidic media, the amount of H + for conversion of the dinitrogen was enhanced, so that in pH 3, the highest concentration of NH3 has been generated. However, in a more acidic solution (pH = 2), through reducing the nanocomposite durability, the NH3 production was alleviated. In alkaline media, in contrast to the acidic media, the amount of proton was diminished, and NH3 generation was reduced [71].

Durability is another significant factor in evaluating the photocatalysts for practical applications. When doing four recycles (Fig. 10
), no impressive variation can be seen in the gained NH3 yields, demonstrating the excellent recycling durability. Furthermore, to demonstrate the durability, the XRD pattern of the ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) before and after 4th recycles has been determined (Fig. 10b). It observed that the structure and phase of the optimum sample is stable, which demonstrates that the ternary system has an excellent durability during the N2 fixation reaction. Besides, the changes of morphology of the photocatalyst before and after reaction were observed by SEM. As seen in Fig. 10 (c, d), the morphology of ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) catalyst does not change significantly. In sum, all these outcomes, strongly support the possibility of applying ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs (0.5 mL) system as a robust and effective photocatalyst for N2 fixation.

A ZnO/ZnSnO3/CDs system was constructed through the hydrothermal procedure and, firstly, utilized in photocatalytic converting of nitrogen to ammonia. The adding of ZnSnO3 and CDs demonstrates a remarkably effect on the BET surface area, charge carriers separation efficiency, and light absorption ability. Therefore, the photocatalytic N2 fixation activity under visible light excitation is much improved. The nanocomposite through adhering 0.5 mL of CDs over ZnO/ZnSnO3 presented the maximum NH3 generation rate, which was 11.5, and 2.1-folds premier than the ZnO and ZnO/ZnSnO3 (30%) systems, respectively. Applying an electron scavenger and two aprotic solvents affirmed that electrons and protons are the major reactive species in the N2 fixation reaction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Mohsen Mohammadi acknowledges the funding received from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) Grant No. RGPIN-2016–04221. Mehdi Shahedi Asl thanks the McCain Foundation for providing enough funding through the McCain Foundation Postdoctoral Fellowship in Innovation program to conduct this work. This research was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (2020M2D8A206983011). The financial support of the Basic Science Research Program (2017R1A2B3009135) through the National Research Foundation of Korea is appreciated. In addition, this work was funded by Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number of 104.05–2020.15.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2021.01.012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile fabrication of g-C3N4 QDs/BiVO4 Z-scheme heterojunction towards enhancing photodegradation activity under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, H. Che, C. Liu, G. Che, P.A. Charpentier, W.Z. Xu, X. Wang, L. Liu, Facile fabrication of g-C3N4 QDs/BiVO4 Z-scheme heterojunction towards enhancing photodegradation activity under visible light, J Taiwan Inst Chem Eng95 (2019) 669–81.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile construction of porous intramolecular g-C3N4-based donor-acceptor conjugated copolymers as highly efficient photocatalysts for superior H2 evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Che, C. Liu, G. Che, G. Liao, H. Dong, C. Li, N. Song, C. Li, Facile construction of porous intramolecular g-C3N4-based donor-acceptor conjugated copolymers as highly efficient photocatalysts for superior H2 evolution, Nano Energy67 (2020) 104273.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light-driven CQDs@MIL-125 (Ti) nanocomposite photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of tetracycline

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, G. Che, W. Jiang, L. Liu, H. Wang, Visible-light-driven CQDs@MIL-125 (Ti) nanocomposite photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of tetracycline, RSC Adv9 (2019) 33238–45.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insight into the activity and stability of RhxP nano-species supported on g-C3N4 for photocatalytic H2 production

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Z-scheme mesoporous photocatalyst constructed by modification of Sn3O4 nanoclusters on g-C3N4 nanosheets with improved photocatalytic performance and mechanism insight
